Grade 11 English Module · Analytical Exposition

CHAPTER 2 — EXPOSITION TEXT

Environmental Responsibility in Island Life

“Plastic Kills the Sea: Our Island is Drowning in Waste”

Protecting Our School, Our Coral Reefs, and Our Future

IsleLearn active reading

Baca isu lingkungan ini sebagai bahan argumen, bukan sekadar teks panjang.

Gunakan modul ini untuk menemukan tesis, alasan, bukti, counterargument, dan tindakan nyata yang dekat dengan sekolah dan pulau.

69bagian belajar
10gambar tertanam
XIFase F

1. Chapter Identity

Komponen Keterangan
Mata Pelajaran Bahasa Inggris
Kelas/Fase Kelas XI / Fase F
Genre Analytical Exposition Text
Tema Environmental Responsibility in Island Life
Judul Plastic Kills the Sea: Our Island is Drowning in Waste
Subtema Protecting Our Sea from Plastic Waste, Illegal Fishing, and Coral Damage
Konteks SMA Negeri 6 Pangkajene dan Kepulauan, Pulau Sapuka, Liukang Tangaya, Pangkep
Fokus Isu Sampah plastik sekolah, sampah pulau, illegal fishing, destructive fishing, coral damage, tanggung jawab bersama
Pendekatan Genre-Based Approach/SFL, CLT, Deep Learning, Environmental Literacy, Project-Based Learning
Produk Akhir Analytical exposition text 400–700 kata + mini debate + environmental campaign project
Skill Utama Listening, Speaking/Debate, Reading/Viewing, Writing/Presenting
Language Focus Simple present, causal connectors, hedging, nominalisation, persuasive language

2. Learning Mission

In this chapter, students learn how to use English to speak about real environmental problems in island life. The focus is not only on understanding exposition text, but also on using arguments, evidence, and responsible language to protect the school environment and the sea.

Students will listen to a mini podcast, read exposition texts, analyze thesis and arguments, discuss responsibility, join a mini debate, write argument paragraphs, and create an environmental campaign.

Core message:

An island cannot be healthy if the school is dirty and the sea is damaged.

3. Situational Hook

Read and Think

Every day after the school bell rings, the yard fills with plastic cups and snack wrappers — but who is responsible for what happens next?

After break time, some students return to class. Some carry their books. Some laugh with friends. Some leave plastic cups, straws, and snack wrappers on the ground. At first, the problem may look small. It may look like only a dirty corner near the classroom or the canteen.

But on a small island, waste does not always stay where it is thrown. The wind can move light plastic cups. Rainwater can carry wrappers to lower areas. When the tide is high, waste near the coast can move closer to the sea. Slowly, a school waste problem can become an island waste problem.

The sea also faces another danger. Some fishing practices damage coral reefs and fish habitats. Destructive fishing, including fishing with explosives or poison, can harm the places where fish live and grow. When coral reefs are damaged, the future of island communities is also threatened.

Plastic waste may slowly damage the island. Destructive fishing can damage the sea even faster. Both problems raise the same question:

Who is responsible for protecting our school, our sea, and our future?

4. Tujuan Pembelajaran

Setelah mempelajari chapter ini, peserta didik mampu:

  1. memahami fungsi sosial analytical exposition text;
  2. mengidentifikasi struktur analytical exposition: thesis, arguments, dan reiteration;
  3. menemukan main idea dan supporting evidence dalam teks eksposisi;
  4. membedakan opinion, argument, evidence, example, dan explanation;
  5. menggunakan simple present tense untuk menyatakan fakta umum, kebiasaan, dan pendapat;
  6. menggunakan causal connectors seperti because, therefore, as a result, for this reason, consequently, dan this leads to;
  7. mengenali dan menggunakan hedging expressions seperti studies show, it is argued that, many people believe, this suggests that, dan it may cause;
  8. memahami nominalisation seperti pollution, destruction, consumption, responsibility, reduction, dan protection;
  9. menyampaikan opini dengan argumen yang logis secara lisan;
  10. mengikuti mini debate tentang tanggung jawab menjaga lingkungan sekolah dan laut;
  11. menulis argument paragraph dengan thesis/main claim yang jelas;
  12. menulis analytical exposition text singkat tentang sampah plastik, destructive fishing, atau perlindungan karang;
  13. membuat kampanye sederhana untuk mengurangi sampah plastik dan menjaga laut;
  14. merefleksikan peran pribadi dalam menjaga lingkungan sekolah, pulau, dan laut.

5. Big Questions

  1. Who is responsible for plastic waste at school?
  2. Why does plastic waste matter more on a small island?
  3. How can school waste affect the sea?
  4. Why are coral reefs important for island communities?
  5. How does destructive fishing harm the future of fishermen?
  6. Should students speak up about environmental problems?
  7. Should the canteen reduce plastic cups and wrappers?
  8. How can evidence make our opinion stronger?
  9. How can English help us speak for our island?
  10. What local action can protect both our school and our sea?

PART A — BKoF: Building Knowledge of the Field

6. Observe the Situation

Perhatikan atau bayangkan situasi berikut.

A school yard after break time. Several plastic cups are left near the classroom. Snack wrappers are blown by the wind. Some students walk past them. The canteen is still busy. Far from the yard, the sea surrounds the island.

Diskusikan:

  1. What do you see in the situation?
  2. Why do students leave plastic waste after break time?
  3. What can happen if the waste is not collected?
  4. Why is plastic waste a serious problem on a small island?
  5. Who should take action first?

7. Think–Pair–Share

Jawab sendiri, diskusikan dengan teman, lalu bagikan ke kelas.

Question 1

What plastic waste do students often see at school?

Useful words:

  • plastic cups
  • straws
  • snack wrappers
  • plastic bags
  • bottles
  • food packaging

Question 2

Who is responsible for reducing plastic waste?

Possible answers:

  • students
  • canteen sellers
  • teachers
  • school leaders
  • cleaning staff
  • families
  • everyone

Question 3

What environmental problem also happens in the sea?

Possible answers:

  • illegal fishing
  • destructive fishing
  • coral damage
  • fewer fish
  • dirty coast
  • plastic in the sea

8. Agree or Disagree

Tentukan apakah kamu setuju, tidak setuju, atau belum yakin. Berikan alasan sederhana.

Statement Agree / Disagree / Not Sure Reason
Students are the only people responsible for school plastic waste.
The canteen should reduce plastic cups and wrappers.
Plastic waste in a small island is more serious than in a big city.
Destructive fishing should be stopped because it damages coral reefs.
Students can help protect the sea even if they are not fishermen.
A zero waste challenge can change student habits.

9. Mini Class Survey

Gunakan data nyata jika memungkinkan. Jika belum ada data nyata, gunakan tabel ini sebagai contoh latihan.

Survey A — Plastic Cups at School

Question Class Data
How many students bought drinks in plastic cups today?
How many plastic cups were found after break time?
Where were most plastic cups found?
How many students brought tumblers?
What can we conclude?

Sample Observation Data for Practice

This is sample classroom data, not official school data.

Area Plastic Cups Found Snack Wrappers Found
Near canteen 32 41
School yard 18 25
Near classroom 21 37
Near the gate 9 14
Total 80 117

Discuss:

  1. Which area has the most plastic cups?
  2. Which area has the most snack wrappers?
  3. What does the data suggest?
  4. How can this data support an argument?

10. Environmental Issue Sorting

Kelompokkan isu berikut.

Issue School Environment Sea Environment Both
plastic cups
snack wrappers
dirty school yard
coral reef destruction
destructive fishing
reusable tumblers
ocean pollution
zero waste challenge
fish habitat loss
student campaign

11. Vocabulary Preview

English Bahasa Indonesia Example
plastic waste sampah plastik Plastic waste is a serious problem on small islands.
single-use plastic plastik sekali pakai Single-use plastic creates waste after one use.
littering membuang sampah sembarangan Littering makes the school yard dirty.
marine life kehidupan laut Plastic waste can harm marine life.
coral reef terumbu karang Coral reefs protect fish habitats.
destructive fishing penangkapan ikan yang merusak Destructive fishing damages the sea.
responsibility tanggung jawab Environmental responsibility belongs to everyone.
reduction pengurangan Plastic reduction can start at school.
protection perlindungan Sea protection needs community action.
evidence bukti Strong arguments need evidence.

PART B — HEAR: Mini Podcast Listening

12. Mini Podcast: “Where Does Our Waste Go, and What Happens to Our Sea?”

Pre-Listening Questions

Diskusikan sebelum mendengarkan.

  1. Where does school waste go after students throw it away?
  2. Can plastic waste from school reach the sea?
  3. Why are coral reefs important for fish?
  4. What happens when people use destructive fishing practices?
  5. Who should protect the island environment?

Podcast Script

Host: Welcome to Island Youth Voice. Today, we are talking about a problem that looks small but can become serious: plastic waste and sea damage on small islands.

Every day, students buy drinks and snacks at school. Many drinks come in plastic cups. Many snacks come in plastic wrappers. After break time, some of these cups and wrappers are left in the school yard, near the classroom, or around the canteen.

On a small island, waste can move easily. A plastic cup is light, so the wind can carry it. A snack wrapper can be pushed by rainwater. If waste reaches the coast, it may enter the sea. As a result, a dirty school yard can become part of a bigger ocean problem.

Plastic waste is not the only environmental issue. The sea also faces destructive fishing. Some people use harmful methods that damage coral reefs and fish habitats. We will not discuss how those methods work, but we need to understand the impact. When coral reefs are damaged, small fish lose safe places to live and grow. This can affect fishermen, families, and future generations.

Many people believe that environmental problems are too big for students. However, this is not completely true. Students can start with simple actions: putting waste in the right place, bringing tumblers, reducing single-use plastic, joining school campaigns, and speaking up about sea protection.

The real question is not only, “Who made the waste?” The better question is, “Who will take responsibility?” Students, the canteen, the school, families, fishermen, and the community all have roles to play.

Our island is our home. If we want a clean school and a healthy sea, we must begin with daily habits and shared responsibility.

Host: This is Island Youth Voice. Think deeply, speak clearly, and protect your island.

While-Listening Task A — Listen for Main Ideas

Centang ide yang kamu dengar.

  • Plastic cups and snack wrappers are common school waste.
  • Waste on a small island can move easily.
  • Plastic waste can reach the coast or sea.
  • Destructive fishing can damage coral reefs.
  • Students cannot do anything about environmental problems.
  • Environmental responsibility belongs to many people.

While-Listening Task B — Listen for Cause and Effect

Complete the table.

Cause Effect
A plastic cup is light.
Rainwater pushes wrappers.
Waste reaches the sea.
Coral reefs are damaged.
Students bring tumblers.

While-Listening Task C — Listen for Opinion and Evidence

Opinion / Claim Supporting Reason or Example
Plastic waste is serious on small islands.
Destructive fishing harms the sea.
Students can help protect the environment.
Responsibility should be shared.

Post-Listening Discussion

  1. What part of the podcast is closest to your school life?
  2. What argument from the podcast is the strongest?
  3. What action can students do this week?
  4. What issue is more urgent: plastic waste or destructive fishing? Why?

PART C — Understanding Analytical Exposition

13. Social Function of Analytical Exposition

Analytical exposition is a text that presents a writer’s opinion about an issue and supports it with arguments.

The social function is to:

  1. persuade readers;
  2. present a clear position;
  3. support an opinion with arguments;
  4. make readers think about an issue;
  5. encourage responsibility or action.

In this chapter, exposition text helps students speak about environmental responsibility in island life.

A strong exposition does not only say:

“Plastic is bad.”

It explains:

“Plastic waste is dangerous because it can pollute the school environment, reach the sea, harm marine life, and teach careless habits.”

14. Text Structure of Analytical Exposition

Structure Function Guiding Questions Example
Thesis States the writer’s position What is my opinion? What do I believe? Our school should reduce plastic cups because they create waste and harm the island environment.
Arguments Give reasons and evidence Why do I believe this? What evidence supports it? Plastic cups are light; therefore, wind can carry them to other areas.
Reiteration Restates the position strongly What should readers remember or do? For these reasons, reducing plastic cups must become a shared school responsibility.

Important Differences

Concept Meaning Example
Topic The subject being discussed Plastic waste at school
Opinion What the writer believes Plastic waste is a serious problem.
Argument A reason supporting the opinion Plastic waste can make the school dirty and may reach the sea.
Evidence Example/data/fact that supports the argument In a class observation, students found plastic cups near the canteen.
Explanation How the evidence supports the argument This shows that student habits create visible waste after break time.
Reiteration Restatement of the thesis Therefore, the school community must reduce single-use plastic.

PART D — SEE: Model Text 1

15. Main Model Text

Plastic Kills the Sea: Our Island is Drowning in Waste

Every day after the school bell rings, the yard fills with plastic cups and snack wrappers. Some students walk past them as if the waste belongs to nobody. Some cups stay near the canteen. Some wrappers move with the wind. At first, this may look like a small school problem. However, on a small island like Sapuka, plastic waste is not only a school issue. It is also an island and sea issue. Our school community should reduce plastic waste and take part in protecting the sea because school waste can pollute the environment, reach the coast, harm marine life, and teach careless habits.

The first reason is that plastic waste makes the school environment dirty and unhealthy. A school is not only a place for studying subjects; it is also a place for building habits. When students throw plastic cups and snack wrappers on the ground, they learn that cleanliness is someone else’s responsibility. This habit is dangerous because it slowly becomes normal. If students get used to leaving waste after break time, they may do the same thing at home, near the beach, or on the boat. In a sample class observation, students may find dozens of plastic cups and snack wrappers around the canteen and school yard in only one day. This kind of observation suggests that school waste is not an invisible problem. It is real, daily, and close to students’ lives.

The second reason is that plastic waste can move from the school environment to wider areas. On a small island, space is limited and the sea is always close. Plastic cups are light; therefore, the wind can carry them easily. Snack wrappers can be pushed by rainwater. If waste is left near open areas, it may move toward lower places, roads, drainage paths, or the coast. As a result, waste that begins in the school yard can become part of a beach or ocean problem. This is why students should not think, “It is only one cup.” One cup may look small, but repeated careless actions can create a serious environmental habit.

The third reason is that plastic pollution can harm the sea and the people who depend on it. The sea around island communities is not only beautiful scenery. It is a source of food, work, and identity. Many families depend on fish, seaweed, lobster, and other marine resources. When plastic waste reaches the sea, it can disturb marine life and make the coast look dirty. Plastic pollution may also break into smaller pieces over time. This can create long-term environmental problems. Even if students do not see the damage immediately, the waste they throw away today can become part of a bigger problem tomorrow. Therefore, reducing plastic waste at school is also a way of respecting the sea.

The fourth reason is that environmental responsibility in Sapuka must include the protection of coral reefs and fish habitats. Plastic waste is not the only danger. The sea can also be damaged by destructive fishing practices, including fishing with explosives or poison. These practices should be rejected because they damage coral reefs, destroy fish habitats, and threaten the future of fishermen. Coral reefs are important because many small fish live and grow there. If reefs are damaged, fish populations may decline, and fishermen may find it harder to catch fish in the future. It is argued that protecting coral reefs means protecting the long-term life of island communities. Students do not need to become fishermen to care about this issue. They can learn, speak, write, and campaign for sea protection.

The fifth reason is that local solutions can start from school. A school does not have to wait for a perfect program to begin. Students can bring tumblers, reduce single-use plastic, put waste in the right place, and remind friends respectfully. The canteen can support change by reducing plastic cups and offering better alternatives. The school can create a zero waste challenge, provide clear waste bins, and give appreciation to classes that keep their areas clean. Students can also create posters, podcasts, short speeches, and English campaign texts about protecting the sea and coral reefs. These actions may look simple, but they can build a culture of responsibility.

Some people may argue that students are too young to solve environmental problems. However, this view is not fair. Students may not be able to solve all island waste problems or stop destructive fishing alone, but they can start a change in the space they know best: their classroom, school yard, canteen, and peer group. Small actions become stronger when they are repeated by many people. A clean school can teach clean habits. A student campaign can influence friends. A simple English paragraph can become a voice for the island.

For these reasons, plastic waste, destructive fishing, and coral damage should not be ignored. Sapuka needs students, schools, families, canteens, fishermen, and communities to share responsibility. Our island cannot be healthy if the school is dirty and the sea is damaged. Reducing plastic waste at school and protecting coral reefs are not separate actions. They are part of the same responsibility: protecting our home, our sea, and our future.

16. Mini Glossary

Word/Phrase Arti Example
yard halaman The yard fills with plastic cups.
wrapper bungkus Snack wrappers move with the wind.
pollute mencemari Plastic waste can pollute the environment.
careless ceroboh/tidak peduli Careless habits create waste problems.
observation observasi A class observation can show waste data.
marine life kehidupan laut Plastic pollution can harm marine life.
destructive fishing penangkapan ikan yang merusak Destructive fishing damages coral reefs.
fish habitat habitat ikan Coral reefs are fish habitats.
reduce mengurangi Students should reduce plastic waste.
shared responsibility tanggung jawab bersama Sea protection is a shared responsibility.

17. Comprehension Questions

  1. What is the main thesis of the text?
  2. Why does the writer say plastic waste is not only a school problem?
  3. How can plastic waste move from the school yard to the sea?
  4. Why is the sea important for island communities?
  5. What is the connection between destructive fishing and coral reefs?
  6. Why does the writer mention students, canteens, schools, fishermen, and communities?
  7. What local solutions are suggested in the text?
  8. Which argument do you think is the strongest? Why?

18. Main Idea and Evidence Hunt

Complete the table based on the text.

Paragraph Main Idea Supporting Evidence / Example Connector Used
1
2
3
4
5
6
7

19. Annotated Text Analysis

Paragraph Structure Main Idea Writer’s Purpose
1 Thesis School plastic waste is an island and sea issue. To state the writer’s position.
2 Argument 1 Plastic waste makes the school dirty and builds careless habits. To show the school-level problem.
3 Argument 2 Waste can move from school to wider areas. To connect school waste with island geography.
4 Argument 3 Plastic pollution can harm marine life and local livelihoods. To show environmental and social impact.
5 Argument 4 Destructive fishing damages coral reefs and fish habitats. To expand the issue to sea protection.
6 Argument 5 Local solutions can start from school. To give practical action.
7 Reiteration Environmental protection is shared responsibility. To restate and strengthen the thesis.

PART E — Main Idea and Supporting Evidence Lab

20. Understanding the Difference

Element Meaning Example
Topic What the paragraph is about Plastic waste at school
Opinion What someone believes Plastic waste is a serious problem.
Argument A reason that supports the opinion It makes the school dirty and may reach the sea.
Evidence A fact, data, observation, or example Students found many plastic cups near the canteen.
Explanation How the evidence proves the argument This shows that plastic waste is produced daily by school habits.

21. Example Analysis A — Plastic Waste

Element Example
Opinion Plastic waste is a serious problem for our school.
Argument It makes the school environment dirty and unhealthy.
Evidence Students often leave plastic cups and snack wrappers after break time.
Explanation When waste is left outside, it can attract insects, look unpleasant, and teach careless habits.
Link Back Therefore, reducing plastic waste must become a school priority.

22. Example Analysis B — Destructive Fishing

Element Example
Opinion Destructive fishing should be stopped.
Argument It damages coral reefs and fish habitats.
Evidence Coral reefs are places where many small fish live and grow.
Explanation When reefs are damaged, fish populations may decline and fishermen can lose future income.
Link Back For this reason, protecting coral reefs means protecting island life.

23. Practice — Strong or Weak Argument?

Decide whether each argument is strong or weak. Improve the weak ones.

  1. Plastic is bad because I do not like it.
  2. Plastic cups should be reduced because they create waste after one use.
  3. Destructive fishing should stop because it is not good.
  4. Coral reefs must be protected because they provide habitat for many marine animals.
  5. Students should bring tumblers because it can reduce single-use plastic at school.
  6. The canteen is the only problem because students buy snacks there.

Revision space:

  • Weak argument 1 revised: ____________________________________
  • Weak argument 2 revised: ____________________________________
  • Weak argument 3 revised: ____________________________________

PART F — Grammar and Language Lab

24. Simple Present for Facts and Opinions

Function

Simple present tense is used in exposition text to talk about facts, habits, general truths, and opinions.

Pattern

Sentence Type Pattern Example
Positive Subject + Verb 1/s/es Plastic waste harms the sea.
Negative Subject + do/does not + Verb 1 A clean school does not happen without responsibility.
Question Do/Does + Subject + Verb 1? Does plastic waste affect marine life?

Examples

  1. Plastic waste harms the environment.
  2. Students throw away plastic cups after break time.
  3. Snack wrappers make the school yard dirty.
  4. The canteen sells drinks in plastic cups.
  5. Small islands face bigger waste problems because space is limited.
  6. Destructive fishing damages coral reefs.
  7. Coral reefs protect marine life.
  8. Many fish depend on healthy reefs.
  9. A zero waste challenge encourages better habits.
  10. Environmental responsibility belongs to everyone.
  11. The school needs a stronger waste system.
  12. Students need to bring tumblers more often.
  13. Plastic pollution affects the beauty of the coast.
  14. Sea protection starts with daily habits.
  15. A clean school reflects responsible students.

Common Mistakes

Incorrect Correct Explanation
Plastic waste harm the sea. Plastic waste harms the sea. Singular subject needs -s.
Students throws plastic cups. Students throw plastic cups. Plural subject uses Verb 1.
The canteen sell drinks. The canteen sells drinks. Singular subject needs -s.
Destructive fishing damage coral reefs. Destructive fishing damages coral reefs. Singular concept needs -s.

Practice

Choose the correct verb.

  1. Plastic waste ________ the school yard dirty. (make / makes)
  2. Students ________ snacks during break time. (buy / buys)
  3. Coral reefs ________ fish habitats. (protect / protects)
  4. Destructive fishing ________ the sea. (damage / damages)
  5. A zero waste challenge ________ students to change. (encourage / encourages)

Use It in an Argument

Complete the argument.

Plastic waste ________ our school environment because ____________________. Therefore, students should ____________________.

25. Causal Connectors

Function

Causal connectors show cause and effect. They help writers explain why an issue happens and what result it creates.

Common Connectors

Connector Meaning Example
because karena Plastic waste is dangerous because it can reach the sea.
therefore oleh karena itu Plastic cups are light; therefore, wind can carry them.
as a result akibatnya Waste is left outside; as a result, the yard becomes dirty.
for this reason karena alasan ini Coral reefs protect fish; for this reason, they must be protected.
consequently akibatnya Reefs are damaged; consequently, fish lose habitats.
this leads to ini menyebabkan High plastic use leads to more waste.
this causes ini menyebabkan Littering causes environmental problems.

Examples in Local Context

  1. Students often buy drinks in plastic cups because they are cheap and practical.
  2. Plastic cups are light; therefore, the wind can carry them easily.
  3. Snack wrappers are left near the classroom; as a result, the school looks dirty.
  4. Small islands have limited space; for this reason, waste management is important.
  5. Destructive fishing damages coral reefs; consequently, fish lose safe habitats.
  6. Coral reefs are important because many small fish live there.
  7. High plastic consumption leads to more school waste.
  8. Littering causes dirty yards and unhealthy habits.
  9. Plastic waste may reach the coast; therefore, students must reduce it.
  10. The canteen uses too many plastic cups; as a result, waste increases after break time.

Common Mistakes

Incorrect Correct
Plastic is dangerous because can reach the sea. Plastic is dangerous because it can reach the sea.
Therefore students throw waste. Students throw waste; therefore, the yard becomes dirty.
As a result students should bring tumbler. As a result, students should bring tumblers.

Practice

Combine the sentences using the connector in brackets.

  1. Plastic cups are light. Wind can carry them. (therefore)
  2. Students leave wrappers after break time. The school yard becomes dirty. (as a result)
  3. Coral reefs are damaged. Fish lose habitat. (consequently)
  4. Students bring tumblers. Plastic cup waste decreases. (because)
  5. The sea supports many families. We must protect it. (for this reason)

Use It in an Argument

Write two sentences using because, therefore, or as a result.

26. Hedging for Careful Arguments

Function

Hedging means using careful language. It helps writers avoid overclaiming. In exposition text, hedging makes arguments sound more academic and responsible.

Common Hedging Expressions

Expression Meaning Example
studies show that penelitian menunjukkan bahwa Studies show that plastic pollution can harm marine animals.
it is argued that ada pendapat bahwa It is argued that schools should reduce single-use plastic.
many people believe that banyak orang percaya bahwa Many people believe that destructive fishing threatens island communities.
this suggests that ini menunjukkan bahwa This suggests that student habits can affect the environment.
it may cause ini dapat menyebabkan Plastic waste may cause long-term pollution.
it can lead to ini dapat menyebabkan Coral damage can lead to fish habitat loss.

Examples

  1. Studies show that plastic pollution can harm marine animals.
  2. It is argued that schools should reduce single-use plastic.
  3. Many people believe that destructive fishing threatens the future of island communities.
  4. This suggests that student habits and community practices can affect the environment.
  5. Plastic waste may cause long-term environmental problems.
  6. Coral reef damage can lead to fewer fish habitats.
  7. It is often argued that environmental education should begin at school.
  8. This suggests that a zero waste challenge can build better habits.
  9. Many people believe that everyone has a role in protecting the sea.
  10. It may be difficult to change habits, but small actions can start the process.

Common Mistakes

Too Strong / Weak Better Academic Style
Plastic always kills all fish. Plastic pollution can harm marine life.
All students are careless. Some students may still need stronger waste awareness.
The canteen is the only problem. The canteen may contribute to plastic waste, but students and the school also have roles.
Fishing destroys everything. Destructive fishing can damage coral reefs and fish habitats.

Practice

Rewrite these sentences using hedging.

  1. Plastic destroys the sea.
  2. Students are careless.
  3. The canteen causes all waste.
  4. Illegal fishing ruins the future.
  5. School campaigns solve everything.

27. Nominalisation

Function

Nominalisation changes verbs or adjectives into nouns. It makes writing sound more formal and academic.

Examples

Verb/Adjective Noun Example
pollute pollution Plastic pollution harms the sea.
destroy destruction Coral reef destruction affects fish habitats.
consume consumption High plastic consumption creates waste.
reduce reduction Plastic reduction can start at school.
protect protection Sea protection needs community action.
responsible responsibility Environmental responsibility belongs to everyone.
conserve conservation Marine conservation is important for island life.
damage damage/destruction Coral damage can affect fish populations.
educate education Environmental education should begin at school.
prevent prevention Waste prevention is better than cleanup.

Transformation Practice

Change the sentence into a more formal sentence using nominalisation.

  1. Plastic pollutes the sea.
    → Plastic pollution damages the sea.

  2. People consume too much single-use plastic.
    → High plastic consumption creates waste problems.

  3. People destroy coral reefs.
    → Coral reef destruction harms marine life.

  4. Students reduce plastic use.
    → Plastic reduction starts with students.

  5. Communities protect the sea.
    → Sea protection needs community action.

Practice

Rewrite these sentences using nominalisation.

  1. Students should reduce plastic cups.
  2. People should protect coral reefs.
  3. The community must prevent destructive fishing.
  4. Schools should educate students about waste.
  5. People should conserve marine life.

28. Thesis Statement Writing

A thesis statement tells the reader your opinion clearly.

Weak Thesis vs Strong Thesis

Weak Thesis Strong Thesis
Plastic is bad. Our school should reduce plastic cups because they create waste, harm the environment, and teach careless habits.
The sea is important. Coral reefs must be protected because they support marine life and the future of island communities.
Students should be clean. Students should stop littering because school waste can damage the environment and shape bad habits.
Fishing is a problem. Destructive fishing should be stopped because it damages coral reefs, reduces fish habitats, and threatens fishermen’s future.

Thesis Formula

Issue + Position + Reasons

Example:

Plastic waste in Sapuka is not only a school problem; it is an island problem because it can pollute the yard, reach the sea, and harm marine life.

Practice

Write a thesis statement for each topic.

  1. Students should bring tumblers.
    Thesis: ___________________________________________________

  2. The canteen should reduce plastic cups.
    Thesis: ___________________________________________________

  3. Destructive fishing should be stopped.
    Thesis: ___________________________________________________

  4. Coral reefs must be protected.
    Thesis: ___________________________________________________

  5. Zero waste challenge should be started at school.
    Thesis: ___________________________________________________

29. Argument Paragraph Pattern

Pattern

Main claim → Reason → Evidence/Example → Explanation → Link back to thesis

Model Paragraph 1 — Plastic Cups

Plastic cups should be reduced at school because they create daily waste after break time. Many students buy drinks in plastic cups because they are cheap and practical. However, after the drinks are finished, the cups often become waste around the canteen or school yard. In a sample class observation, students may find many plastic cups in only one day. This shows that small daily habits can create a visible waste problem. Therefore, reducing plastic cups is an important step toward a cleaner school.

Model Paragraph 2 — Coral Reefs

Destructive fishing should be stopped because it damages coral reefs and fish habitats. Coral reefs are important places where many small fish live and grow. When harmful fishing practices damage reefs, fish lose safe places to hide and reproduce. As a result, fishermen may face fewer fish in the future. This means that coral reef protection is not only an environmental issue but also a community issue.

Guided Practice

Complete the paragraph.

Plastic waste can reach the sea because ____________________________. For example, ____________________________. This means that ____________________________. Therefore, ____________________________.

PART G — Vocabulary in Use

30. Vocabulary Clusters

A. Plastic Waste Vocabulary

Word/Phrase Arti Example
plastic cup gelas plastik Students should reduce plastic cups.
snack wrapper bungkus makanan ringan Snack wrappers are often found after break time.
single-use plastic plastik sekali pakai Single-use plastic creates waste quickly.
waste sampah/limbah Waste must be managed properly.
litter sampah berserakan Litter makes the school dirty.
trash bin tempat sampah Students should use trash bins.
landfill tempat pembuangan akhir Small islands may have limited landfill space.
microplastic mikroplastik Microplastic can become a long-term problem.
reusable bottle botol pakai ulang A reusable bottle can reduce waste.
tumbler botol minum Students can bring tumblers to school.
plastic straw sedotan plastik Plastic straws are often used once.
food packaging kemasan makanan Food packaging often becomes waste.
waste sorting pemilahan sampah Waste sorting helps recycling.
cleanup kegiatan membersihkan A cleanup can reduce visible waste.
waste reduction pengurangan sampah Waste reduction should start at school.

B. Environmental Impact Vocabulary

Word/Phrase Arti Example
pollution pencemaran Pollution damages the environment.
marine life kehidupan laut Marine life needs a clean sea.
ecosystem ekosistem A healthy ecosystem supports many species.
coral reef terumbu karang Coral reefs protect fish habitats.
fish habitat habitat ikan Destructive fishing destroys fish habitats.
coast pantai/pesisir Waste may move toward the coast.
shoreline garis pantai Plastic can collect along the shoreline.
harmful berbahaya Plastic waste is harmful to the sea.
dangerous berbahaya Destructive fishing is dangerous for marine life.
long-term damage kerusakan jangka panjang Coral damage can create long-term problems.
marine animal hewan laut Marine animals may be affected by waste.
fish population populasi ikan Fish populations may decline.
natural resource sumber daya alam The sea is an important natural resource.
coastal area wilayah pesisir Coastal areas need protection.
environmental threat ancaman lingkungan Plastic pollution is an environmental threat.

C. Destructive Fishing Vocabulary

Word/Phrase Arti Example
illegal fishing penangkapan ikan ilegal Illegal fishing should be stopped.
destructive fishing penangkapan ikan yang merusak Destructive fishing damages the sea.
explosive fishing penangkapan ikan dengan bahan peledak Explosive fishing can destroy coral reefs.
poison fishing penangkapan ikan dengan racun/bius Poison fishing harms marine life.
coral damage kerusakan karang Coral damage affects fish habitats.
reef destruction perusakan terumbu Reef destruction threatens fishermen’s future.
marine conservation konservasi laut Marine conservation protects island life.
fishing practice praktik penangkapan ikan Harmful fishing practices should be avoided.
community rule aturan masyarakat Community rules can protect the sea.
sea protection perlindungan laut Sea protection needs everyone.
fish nursery tempat tumbuh ikan kecil Coral reefs can act as fish nurseries.
habitat loss hilangnya habitat Habitat loss reduces marine life.
sustainable fishing penangkapan ikan berkelanjutan Sustainable fishing protects the future.
coastal community masyarakat pesisir Coastal communities depend on the sea.
future generation generasi masa depan Future generations need a healthy sea.

D. School Context Vocabulary

Word/Phrase Arti Example
school yard halaman sekolah The school yard should be clean.
canteen kantin The canteen can reduce plastic cups.
break time waktu istirahat Waste increases after break time.
classroom ruang kelas Clean classrooms support learning.
student habit kebiasaan siswa Student habits affect the environment.
cleaning duty piket kebersihan Cleaning duty should not replace responsibility.
school rule aturan sekolah School rules can reduce littering.
campaign kampanye Students can create a waste reduction campaign.
challenge tantangan A zero waste challenge can motivate students.
responsibility tanggung jawab Responsibility belongs to everyone.
school community warga sekolah The school community must work together.
awareness kesadaran Awareness can change habits.
reminder pengingat Posters can be reminders.
class observation observasi kelas Class observation can provide evidence.
student voice suara siswa Student voice can support environmental action.

E. Argument Language

Phrase Arti Example
in my opinion menurut pendapat saya In my opinion, students should bring tumblers.
I believe that saya percaya bahwa I believe that plastic waste should be reduced.
the first reason is alasan pertama adalah The first reason is that plastic cups create waste.
this means that ini berarti bahwa This means that waste is a shared problem.
for example misalnya For example, students can bring reusable bottles.
as a result akibatnya As a result, the yard becomes dirty.
therefore oleh karena itu Therefore, action is needed.
however namun However, students also have responsibility.
in conclusion sebagai kesimpulan In conclusion, the school must act together.
it is important to penting untuk It is important to protect coral reefs.
we should consider kita harus mempertimbangkan We should consider the role of the canteen.
evidence shows bukti menunjukkan Evidence shows that waste increases after break time.
this suggests that ini menunjukkan bahwa This suggests that habits need to change.
for this reason karena alasan ini For this reason, zero waste action is needed.
I strongly believe saya sangat percaya I strongly believe that sea protection starts at school.

F. Responsibility and Solution Vocabulary

Word/Phrase Arti Example
reduce mengurangi We must reduce plastic waste.
reuse menggunakan kembali Students can reuse bottles.
recycle mendaur ulang Recycling can help manage waste.
collect mengumpulkan Students collect waste after break time.
separate memilah We should separate organic and plastic waste.
ban melarang The school may ban some single-use plastic.
encourage mendorong Teachers encourage students to bring tumblers.
remind mengingatkan Friends can remind each other politely.
provide menyediakan The school can provide waste bins.
participate berpartisipasi Students participate in campaigns.
protect melindungi We must protect the sea.
conserve melestarikan Communities should conserve coral reefs.
prevent mencegah We should prevent destructive fishing.
educate mendidik Schools educate students about environment.
support mendukung Families can support waste reduction.

31. Phrase Chunks for Speaking and Writing

Use these chunks to build arguments.

Giving Opinion

  • I believe that __________.
  • In my opinion, __________.
  • I strongly believe that __________.
  • From my point of view, __________.

Giving Reason

  • The first reason is that __________.
  • This is important because __________.
  • One major problem is __________.
  • Another reason is __________.

Giving Evidence or Example

  • For example, __________.
  • Based on our class observation, __________.
  • This can be seen when __________.
  • One example is __________.

Explaining Cause and Effect

  • As a result, __________.
  • Therefore, __________.
  • This leads to __________.
  • Consequently, __________.

Reiteration

  • For these reasons, __________.
  • In conclusion, __________.
  • Therefore, we should __________.
  • This is why __________.

32. Mini Speaking Practice

Complete and say your argument.

  1. I believe that our school should reduce __________ because __________.
  2. The first reason is that __________.
  3. For example, __________.
  4. As a result, __________.
  5. Therefore, __________.

33. Mini Writing Practice

Write five connected sentences using these words:

  • plastic cups
  • school yard
  • because
  • as a result
  • responsibility

Your paragraph:

PART H — More Model Texts

34. Model Text 2 — Easy Level

Students Should Bring Tumblers to School

Students should bring tumblers to school because this simple habit can reduce plastic waste. Many students buy drinks during break time. These drinks often come in plastic cups. After the drinks are finished, the cups become waste. Some students put them in trash bins, but others leave them near the classroom, canteen, or school yard.

Bringing a tumbler is a practical solution. If students bring their own bottles, they do not need to use plastic cups every day. This can reduce the number of cups thrown away after break time. It can also teach students to prepare their needs before going to school.

Some students may think that bringing a tumbler is not important. However, small habits can create big changes when many people do them together. If one class brings tumblers, plastic cup waste can decrease. If many classes do the same, the school can become cleaner.

For these reasons, students should bring tumblers to school. It is a simple action, but it can help reduce plastic waste and build environmental responsibility.

Identify the Thesis

Write the thesis statement:

Comprehension Questions

  1. Why should students bring tumblers?
  2. What happens after students finish drinks in plastic cups?
  3. Why is bringing a tumbler a practical solution?
  4. What can happen if many classes bring tumblers?
  5. What is the moral message of the text?

Main Idea and Evidence Table

Paragraph Main Idea Evidence/Example
1
2
3
4

Speaking Prompt

Tell your partner: Do you agree that students should bring tumblers? Why?

Writing Prompt

Write one paragraph supporting this thesis: “Students should bring tumblers to school.”

35. Model Text 3 — Medium Level

The Canteen Should Reduce Single-Use Plastic

The school canteen should reduce single-use plastic because it plays an important role in student waste habits. Many students buy drinks and snacks from the canteen every day. If most products are sold in plastic cups, plastic bags, or plastic wrappers, the amount of waste will increase quickly after break time.

The first reason is that the canteen influences student choices. Students often choose what is available and practical. If the canteen only provides drinks in plastic cups, students will continue using them. However, if the canteen encourages reusable bottles, refill systems, or less plastic packaging, students may slowly change their habits. This suggests that the canteen is not only a place for buying food but also a place where environmental habits can be shaped.

The second reason is that single-use plastic creates waste after only a few minutes of use. A plastic cup may be used for ten minutes, but it can remain as waste for a very long time. As a result, the school must deal with more trash every day. In a small island school, this is a serious issue because waste space is limited and the sea is close.

The third reason is that canteen policy can support school campaigns. A zero waste challenge will be stronger if the canteen takes part. For example, the canteen can give discounts to students who bring tumblers or reduce unnecessary plastic packaging. This can make environmental action feel real, not only a poster on the wall.

For these reasons, the canteen should reduce single-use plastic. Students must also be responsible, but the canteen can help create better choices. A cleaner school needs shared action from everyone.

Thesis Identification

Thesis: _______________________________________________________

Comprehension Questions

  1. Why does the writer focus on the canteen?
  2. How does the canteen influence student choices?
  3. Why is single-use plastic a problem?
  4. How can the canteen support a zero waste challenge?
  5. Does the writer blame only the canteen? Explain.

Useful Expressions

  • The canteen plays an important role because...
  • If the canteen provides better alternatives,...
  • This suggests that...
  • Shared action is needed because...

Writing Prompt

Write one argument paragraph about the role of the canteen in reducing plastic waste.

36. Model Text 4 — Local Context

Plastic Waste Is an Island Problem

Plastic waste is more serious on small islands because the environment is limited, the sea is close, and daily habits are visible. In big cities, waste may disappear from people’s sight after being collected. On a small island, however, waste can remain near houses, schools, roads, beaches, or coastal areas. For this reason, plastic waste in Sapuka should be seen as an island problem, not only a personal problem.

The first reason is that small islands have limited space. When people use too much single-use plastic, they create waste that must be stored, collected, burned, reused, or transported. None of these options is simple. If waste is not managed well, it can make the environment dirty and unhealthy. This is why reducing waste is better than only cleaning it after it appears.

The second reason is that the sea is close to daily life. Students in Sapuka do not live far from the coast. Many families depend on the sea for fish, seaweed, travel, and work. If plastic waste moves toward the coast, it can affect the beauty of the island and the health of marine life. This means that a plastic cup thrown carelessly at school is not always separated from the sea. In island life, everything is connected.

The third reason is that student habits can influence community habits. A student who learns to put waste in the right place may remind younger siblings or friends. A class that starts a tumbler movement may inspire another class. A school campaign can reach families and the wider community. Therefore, environmental education at school can become a starting point for island change.

In conclusion, plastic waste is an island problem because it affects space, sea, and community habits. Sapuka needs students who understand that protecting the school environment is part of protecting the island.

Main Idea and Evidence Table

Argument Main Idea Evidence/Example
1
2
3

Speaking Prompt

Explain why plastic waste is more serious on a small island than in a big city.

Writing Prompt

Write a paragraph beginning with: “Plastic waste in Sapuka is not only a school problem because...”

37. Model Text 5 — Marine Environment

Destructive Fishing Should Be Stopped

Destructive fishing should be stopped because it damages coral reefs, reduces fish habitats, and threatens the future of island communities. The sea is not only a place where fishermen catch fish. It is a living system. When one part of the system is damaged, other parts can also be affected.

The first reason is that destructive fishing damages coral reefs. Coral reefs are important because they provide shelter and habitat for many marine animals. Small fish can hide, grow, and find food around healthy reefs. If reefs are destroyed, the sea loses an important home for marine life. This can create long-term damage that is difficult to repair.

The second reason is that destructive fishing can reduce future fish populations. Some harmful fishing practices may catch fish quickly, but they can also damage the places where fish reproduce and grow. As a result, fishermen may find fewer fish in the future. This means that destructive fishing is not only an environmental problem. It is also an economic and social problem for families who depend on the sea.

The third reason is that young people need to understand marine conservation. Students may not be the ones who fish every day, but they are part of the island community. They can learn why coral reefs matter, discuss the issue at school, create campaign posters, and remind people that the sea must be protected. Education can help prevent harmful practices from becoming normal.

For these reasons, destructive fishing should be stopped. Protecting coral reefs means protecting fish, fishermen, families, and future generations.

Comprehension Questions

  1. What is the writer’s thesis?
  2. Why are coral reefs important?
  3. How can destructive fishing affect future fish populations?
  4. Why is destructive fishing also a social problem?
  5. What can students do to support marine conservation?

Writing Prompt

Write one argument paragraph about why coral reefs must be protected.

38. Model Text 6 — Coral Protection

Protecting Coral Reefs Means Protecting Our Future

Coral reefs must be protected because they support marine life and the future of island communities. Many people see coral reefs only as beautiful underwater scenery. In fact, reefs are much more than that. They are homes, shelters, and feeding areas for many sea creatures.

The first reason is that coral reefs provide habitat for fish. Many small fish live around reefs because reefs give them places to hide from predators. When reefs are healthy, marine life can grow better. This supports the balance of the sea ecosystem.

The second reason is that coral reefs support fishermen’s future. If reefs are damaged, fish habitats may decline. As a result, fishermen may need to travel farther or work harder to catch fish. This can affect family income and food supply. Therefore, coral reef protection is connected to the daily life of coastal communities.

The third reason is that coral reefs are difficult to restore once they are badly damaged. It may take a long time for damaged reefs to recover. For this reason, prevention is better than repair. Communities should avoid destructive practices, protect reef areas, and teach young people about marine conservation.

In conclusion, protecting coral reefs means protecting marine life, fishermen, and future generations. The sea gives many things to island communities; therefore, communities must also give protection back to the sea.

Thesis Identification

Thesis: _______________________________________________________

Main Idea and Evidence Table

Argument Main Idea Supporting Evidence / Explanation
1
2
3

Speaking Prompt

Say one reason why coral reefs are important for Sapuka or island communities.

Writing Prompt

Write a reiteration for this text using your own words.

PART I — SAY: Mini Debate

39. Debate Topic

Who is responsible for protecting our island environment: students, the school, the canteen, fishermen, or the whole community?

Debate Groups

Group Position
Group A Students are responsible.
Group B The canteen is responsible.
Group C The school is responsible.
Group D Fishermen and coastal communities are responsible.
Group E Everyone is responsible.

Debate Rules

  1. Speak respectfully.
  2. Do not attack people; discuss ideas.
  3. Use reasons and examples.
  4. Listen before responding.
  5. Use English expressions as much as possible.
  6. The goal is not to blame, but to understand shared responsibility.

Debate Roles

Role Task
Opening Speaker States the group’s position.
Argument Speaker Gives reasons and examples.
Evidence Speaker Provides observation, examples, or data.
Rebuttal Speaker Responds to another group politely.
Closing Speaker Summarizes the position.

40. Debate Preparation Sheet

Group Position

We believe that ________________________________________________.

Argument 1

The first reason is that ________________________________________.

Example/Evidence: ______________________________________________.

Argument 2

Another reason is that __________________________________________.

Example/Evidence: ______________________________________________.

Argument 3

The final reason is that ________________________________________.

Example/Evidence: ______________________________________________.

Possible Rebuttal

Some people may say that ________________________________________.

However, we believe that ________________________________________.

41. Useful Debate Expressions

Opening

  • Good morning, everyone. Our group believes that...
  • We strongly believe that...
  • Our position is that...

Giving Argument

  • The main reason is...
  • The first reason is that...
  • Another important reason is...
  • This is important because...

Giving Example

  • For example,...
  • Based on our observation,...
  • This can be seen when...
  • One clear example is...

Rebuttal

  • I understand your point, but...
  • However, we should also consider...
  • That may be true, but...
  • We should not blame only one group because...

Closing

  • Therefore, we strongly believe that...
  • In conclusion,...
  • For these reasons,...
  • Shared responsibility is important because...

42. Reflection After Debate

  1. Which group gave the strongest argument?
  2. What evidence made an argument stronger?
  3. Did your opinion change after the debate?
  4. Why should environmental debate avoid blaming only one group?
  5. What action can the class take after this debate?

PART J — Reading Analysis Practice

43. Identify Thesis Statement

Choose the thesis statement.

  1. Plastic cups are found near the canteen.
  2. Our school should reduce plastic cups because they create waste, harm the environment, and teach careless habits.
  3. Some students buy drinks during break time.

Answer: _______

44. Match Argument with Evidence

Argument Evidence/Example
1. Plastic cups create daily waste. a. Coral reefs are places where small fish live and grow.
2. Coral reefs are important. b. Students found plastic cups near the canteen after break time.
3. Destructive fishing harms future livelihoods. c. Fishermen may find fewer fish if habitats are damaged.
4. A zero waste challenge can change habits. d. Students may bring tumblers and reduce plastic use.

45. Identify Causal Connectors

Underline the causal connectors.

  1. Plastic cups are light; therefore, the wind can carry them easily.
  2. Students leave wrappers after break time; as a result, the school yard becomes dirty.
  3. Coral reefs are important because many small fish live there.
  4. Destructive fishing damages reefs; consequently, fish lose habitats.
  5. High plastic consumption leads to more waste.

46. Identify Hedging Expressions

Circle the hedging expression.

  1. Studies show that plastic pollution can harm marine animals.
  2. It is argued that schools should reduce single-use plastic.
  3. Many people believe that destructive fishing threatens island communities.
  4. This suggests that student habits can affect the environment.
  5. Plastic waste may cause long-term environmental problems.

47. Nominalisation Practice

Change the verbs into nouns.

  1. pollute → __________
  2. destroy → __________
  3. consume → __________
  4. reduce → __________
  5. protect → __________
  6. responsible → __________
  7. conserve → __________
  8. educate → __________

48. Rewrite Weak Arguments

Rewrite these weak arguments into stronger arguments.

  1. Plastic waste is bad because it is ugly.
    Stronger: __________________________________________________

  2. Destructive fishing is bad because people say so.
    Stronger: __________________________________________________

  3. Students must bring tumblers because I like tumblers.
    Stronger: __________________________________________________

  4. Coral reefs are important because they are nice.
    Stronger: __________________________________________________

  5. The canteen should change because it is wrong.
    Stronger: __________________________________________________

PART K — Paragraph Writing Workshop

49. Step-by-Step Argument Paragraph

Step 1 — Write a Main Claim

A main claim is the main idea of your paragraph.

Example:

Plastic cups should be reduced at school.

Your main claim:

Step 2 — Add a Reason

Example:

Plastic cups should be reduced at school because they create daily waste after break time.

Your sentence:

Step 3 — Add Evidence or Example

Example:

For example, many students buy drinks in plastic cups and leave them near the canteen.

Your evidence/example:

Step 4 — Add Explanation

Example:

This shows that plastic cups are used quickly but create waste that the school must manage.

Your explanation:

Step 5 — Link Back to Thesis

Example:

Therefore, reducing plastic cups is an important step toward a cleaner school.

Your link sentence:

50. Full Argument Paragraph Template

Use this template.

Main claim:

Reason:
This is important because ____________________________________.

Evidence/Example:
For example, ________________________________________________.

Explanation:
This means that ______________________________________________.

Link back:
Therefore, _________________________________________________ .

51. Paragraph Models

Model A — School Plastic Waste

Plastic cups should be reduced at school because they create daily waste after break time. Many students buy drinks in plastic cups because they are practical. However, after the drinks are finished, the cups often become trash around the canteen or school yard. This shows that single-use plastic creates a waste problem after only a short use. Therefore, reducing plastic cups can help make the school cleaner.

Model B — Snack Wrappers

Snack wrappers should not be thrown carelessly because they can move easily with the wind. On a small island, light waste does not always stay in one place. For example, wrappers left near the classroom can be carried to the yard, the road, or even toward coastal areas. This means that littering at school can become a wider island problem. For this reason, students must put wrappers in the right place.

Model C — Coral Protection

Coral reefs must be protected because they provide habitat for many fish. Small fish need safe places to live, hide, and grow. When destructive fishing damages reefs, fish may lose their habitats. As a result, fishermen can face fewer fish in the future. Therefore, coral reef protection is important for both marine life and island communities.

PART L — JCoT: Joint Construction

52. Joint Writing Option A

Theme: Our school should reduce plastic cups.

Brainstorming Table

Question Class Ideas
What is the problem?
Why is it serious?
What evidence/example can we use?
Who is responsible?
What solution is realistic?

Thesis Builder

Our school should reduce __________________ because __________________, __________________, and __________________.

Argument Planner

Argument Reason Evidence/Example Connector
1
2
3

Shared Paragraph Example

Our school should reduce plastic cups because they create waste after break time and shape careless habits. Many students buy drinks in plastic cups from the canteen. After the drinks are finished, some cups are left near the classroom or school yard. As a result, the school looks dirty and the waste must be cleaned by others. This shows that plastic reduction is not only about cleanliness but also about responsibility.

53. Joint Writing Option B

Theme: Destructive fishing should be stopped.

Thesis Builder

Destructive fishing should be stopped because __________________, __________________, and __________________.

Class Argument Planner

Argument Supporting Evidence/Example Explanation
It damages coral reefs.
It harms fish habitats.
It threatens future livelihoods.

Shared Paragraph Example

Destructive fishing should be stopped because it damages coral reefs and fish habitats. Coral reefs are important places where many small fish live and grow. When harmful fishing practices damage reefs, fish lose safe places to hide and reproduce. As a result, future fish populations may decline. Therefore, stopping destructive fishing is important for protecting the sea and the future of island communities.

PART M — ICoT: Independent Writing Mission

54. Final Writing Mission

Write an analytical exposition text about an environmental issue in island life.

Choose One Topic

  1. Students should bring tumblers to school.
  2. The school should reduce plastic cups.
  3. The canteen should stop using too much single-use plastic.
  4. Plastic waste in Sapuka is an island problem.
  5. Students must stop throwing snack wrappers carelessly.
  6. A zero waste challenge can change student habits.
  7. Destructive fishing should be stopped.
  8. Coral reefs must be protected for the future of island communities.
  9. Students should join campaigns to protect the sea.

Length

400–700 words.

Required Structure

Structure What to Include
Thesis Your clear opinion and reasons
Argument 1 Main claim + reason + evidence/example + explanation
Argument 2 Main claim + reason + evidence/example + explanation
Argument 3 Main claim + reason + evidence/example + explanation
Reiteration Restate your opinion and final message

Language Checklist

  • I use simple present tense.
  • I use causal connectors.
  • I use at least one hedging expression.
  • I use at least one nominalisation.
  • I use environmental vocabulary.
  • I give examples or evidence.
  • I do not only give opinions without reasons.
  • I end with a strong reiteration.

55. Planning Sheet

Thesis

My thesis is:

Argument 1

Main claim: ________________________________________________

Reason: ____________________________________________________

Evidence/example: ___________________________________________

Explanation: _______________________________________________

Argument 2

Main claim: ________________________________________________

Reason: ____________________________________________________

Evidence/example: ___________________________________________

Explanation: _______________________________________________

Argument 3

Main claim: ________________________________________________

Reason: ____________________________________________________

Evidence/example: ___________________________________________

Explanation: _______________________________________________

Reiteration

For these reasons, __________________________________________.

56. Peer Review Sheet

Read your friend’s draft and give feedback.

Question Yes / Not Yet Suggestion
Does the text have a clear thesis?
Are there at least three arguments?
Does each argument have evidence or example?
Are causal connectors used correctly?
Is the reiteration strong?
Is the topic connected to island life?
What is the strongest part?
What should be improved?

PART N — LRT: Linking Related Texts

57. Related Text Activities

A. Podcast Script

Write a short podcast script about one environmental problem.

Title: ____________________________________

Opening:
Hello, everyone. Today, we are talking about ____________________.

Problem:
This problem happens when ____________________.

Argument:
This is serious because ____________________.

Action:
Students can help by ____________________.

Closing:
Protecting our island means ____________________.

B. Campaign Poster

Create a poster with one of these slogans:

  • Plastic Kills the Sea
  • Bring a Tumbler, Save the Island
  • Protect Our Coral, Protect Our Future
  • Clean School, Healthy Sea
  • Stop Destructive Fishing, Save Marine Life

Your poster must include:

  • one slogan;
  • one problem;
  • two arguments;
  • three actions;
  • one strong closing message.

C. Infographic from Class Waste Data

Use real or sample data.

Title: How Many Plastic Cups Do We Throw Away?

Include:

  • number of plastic cups;
  • where they were found;
  • possible causes;
  • possible effects;
  • solution.

D. Sea Protection Pledge

Complete the pledge.

I believe that our sea is important because ____________________.

I will reduce plastic waste by ____________________.

I will remind my friends to ____________________.

I will support sea protection by ____________________.

My message for my island is ____________________.

PART O — Interactive Quiz Draft

58. Multiple Choice Questions

  1. What is the purpose of analytical exposition text?
    a. To tell a funny story
    b. To describe a person
    c. To present an opinion and support it with arguments
    d. To give cooking steps

  2. The first part of analytical exposition is called...
    a. thesis
    b. complication
    c. orientation
    d. materials

  3. Which sentence is a strong thesis?
    a. Plastic is everywhere.
    b. Our school should reduce plastic cups because they create waste and harm the environment.
    c. Students like drinks.
    d. The canteen is open.

  4. Which connector shows cause and effect?
    a. beautiful
    b. therefore
    c. student
    d. coral

  5. Which word is nominalisation?
    a. protect
    b. pollution
    c. throw
    d. clean

  6. Which practice can damage coral reefs?
    a. bringing tumblers
    b. destructive fishing
    c. reading books
    d. cleaning class

  7. “Plastic waste harms the environment.” This sentence uses...
    a. simple present
    b. simple past
    c. future tense
    d. past continuous

  8. Which sentence uses hedging?
    a. Plastic always kills everything.
    b. Studies show that plastic pollution can harm marine animals.
    c. Students are all careless.
    d. The sea is blue.

  9. What is supporting evidence?
    a. A title
    b. A reason, example, data, or observation that supports an argument
    c. A random opinion
    d. A closing greeting

  10. What is the main message of this chapter?
    a. Only students are guilty.
    b. Environmental protection is shared responsibility.
    c. Plastic cups are beautiful.
    d. Debate is for blaming people.

59. True/False Questions

Write True or False.

  1. Analytical exposition presents an opinion and arguments.
  2. Thesis comes at the end only.
  3. Evidence can make an argument stronger.
  4. Plastic waste at school can be connected to island life.
  5. Destructive fishing can damage coral reefs.
  6. Hedging makes arguments more careful.
  7. Nominalisation makes writing more formal.
  8. Debate should be used to insult other groups.
  9. A zero waste challenge can be a local solution.
  10. Students can use English to speak for their island.

60. Gap-Filling Questions

Complete with suitable words.

  1. Analytical exposition has thesis, arguments, and __________.
  2. Plastic cups and snack wrappers are examples of __________ waste.
  3. Coral reefs are important because they provide fish __________.
  4. The connector “therefore” shows cause and __________.
  5. The noun form of “pollute” is __________.
  6. “Studies show that...” is an example of __________.
  7. A strong argument needs reason and __________.
  8. Destructive fishing should be stopped because it damages __________ reefs.
  9. Students can reduce plastic waste by bringing __________.
  10. Environmental responsibility belongs to __________.

61. Match Argument with Evidence

Argument Evidence/Example
Plastic cups create daily waste. Students find cups near the canteen after break time.
Coral reefs must be protected. Many small fish live and grow around reefs.
The canteen can reduce waste. It can offer refill systems or fewer plastic cups.
Students can support change. They can bring tumblers and join campaigns.
Destructive fishing is harmful. It damages habitats and threatens future fish populations.

PART P — Project / Digital Creation Task

62. Choose One Project

Option A — Campaign Poster

Create a poster titled:

Plastic Kills the Sea

Must include:

  • one slogan;
  • two arguments;
  • three actions;
  • one closing message.

Option B — Class Infographic

Create an infographic titled:

How Many Plastic Cups Do We Throw Away?

Must include:

  • observation data;
  • chart/table;
  • conclusion;
  • solution.

Option C — Zero Waste Challenge Card

Create a challenge card for your class.

Include:

  • challenge name;
  • 5 actions;
  • class target;
  • reward idea;
  • reflection question.

Option D — Sea Protection Poster

Create a poster titled:

Protect Our Coral, Protect Our Future

Must include:

  • why coral reefs matter;
  • one threat;
  • two solutions;
  • one call to action.

Option E — Short Podcast Script

Write a 1–2 minute podcast script about plastic waste or sea protection.

Include:

  • opening;
  • problem;
  • argument;
  • example;
  • action;
  • closing.

Option F — School Announcement

Write a short announcement asking students to reduce plastic waste.

Include:

  • problem;
  • reason;
  • action steps;
  • polite reminder.

PART Q — Reflection

63. Final Reflection

Jawab dengan jujur. Fokus pada kebiasaan dan solusi, bukan saling menyalahkan.

Bahasa Indonesia

  1. Apa kebiasaan saya yang berhubungan dengan sampah plastik di sekolah?
  2. Apakah saya pernah membuang gelas plastik atau bungkus makanan sembarangan?
  3. Bagaimana sampah sekolah dapat memengaruhi laut di pulau saya?
  4. Mengapa karang penting bagi masa depan masyarakat pulau?
  5. Mengapa praktik penangkapan ikan yang merusak harus dihentikan?
  6. Siapa yang seharusnya bertanggung jawab terhadap lingkungan sekolah dan laut?
  7. Apa satu kebiasaan kecil yang bisa saya ubah minggu ini?

English Prompts

Complete the sentences.

  1. I can reduce plastic waste by ____________________.
  2. I can protect the sea by ____________________.
  3. Coral reefs are important because ____________________.
  4. My school can reduce waste by ____________________.
  5. English can help me speak for my island because ____________________.

PART R — Assessment Rubrics

64. Rubric for Exposition Writing

Criteria 4 — Excellent 3 — Good 2 — Developing 1 — Needs Support
Thesis Clarity Thesis is clear, specific, and strongly connected to the issue. Thesis is clear but could be more specific. Thesis is present but unclear. Thesis is missing or confusing.
Argument Quality Arguments are logical, relevant, and convincing. Arguments are mostly logical and relevant. Arguments are weak or repetitive. Arguments are unclear or unsupported.
Supporting Evidence Uses clear examples, observation, or data. Uses some examples. Evidence is limited. No evidence or examples.
Text Structure Thesis, arguments, and reiteration are complete and organized. Structure is mostly complete. Some parts are missing. Structure is difficult to follow.
Grammar Accuracy Uses simple present and sentence patterns accurately most of the time. Some grammar errors but meaning is clear. Frequent errors but still understandable. Errors make meaning difficult.
Vocabulary Use Uses environmental and argument vocabulary effectively. Uses relevant vocabulary with some repetition. Vocabulary is limited. Vocabulary is very limited.
Cohesion/Connectors Uses causal connectors and transitions effectively. Uses some connectors correctly. Connectors are limited or repetitive. Few or no connectors.
Reiteration Strong final restatement and call to action. Clear reiteration. Weak reiteration. Reiteration is missing.

65. Rubric for Mini Debate

Criteria 4 — Excellent 3 — Good 2 — Developing 1 — Needs Support
Opinion Clarity Position is very clear and confident. Position is clear. Position is somewhat unclear. Position is not clear.
Logical Argument Arguments are logical and well-developed. Arguments are relevant. Arguments need more support. Arguments are unclear.
Evidence/Example Uses clear examples or observation. Uses some examples. Examples are limited. No examples.
Speaking Fluency Speaks smoothly and clearly. Speaks with some pauses. Needs frequent pauses. Difficult to understand.
Rebuttal Responds politely and logically. Responds with some logic. Response is limited. No response/rebuttal.
Teamwork Team works together effectively. Team mostly works together. Some teamwork problems. Team is not coordinated.
Respectful Communication Always respectful. Mostly respectful. Needs reminders. Disrespectful communication.

66. Rubric for Environmental Campaign Project

Criteria 4 — Excellent 3 — Good 2 — Developing 1 — Needs Support
Message Clarity Message is clear, strong, and memorable. Message is clear. Message is somewhat unclear. Message is confusing.
Local Relevance Strongly connected to school/sea/island issue. Connected to local issue. Limited local connection. No local connection.
Argument Quality Includes strong reasons and action steps. Includes reasons and action. Reasons are weak. No clear reason/action.
Creativity Creative and engaging. Interesting and neat. Simple but understandable. Minimal effort.
Language Accuracy English is accurate and appropriate. Some errors but clear. Many errors but understandable. Difficult to understand.
Practicality Solution is realistic and doable. Mostly realistic. Needs clearer action. Not practical.

PART S — Teacher Notes

67. Teaching Guidance

A. Discuss Waste Without Shaming Students

Avoid saying:

  • “You are dirty students.”
  • “You do not care about the environment.”
  • “The canteen is the problem.”

Use reflective questions:

  • “What habit creates this problem?”
  • “What can we change together?”
  • “What evidence do we have?”

B. Discuss Destructive Fishing Safely

Do not explain technical methods of harmful fishing practices. Focus only on:

  • environmental impact;
  • coral reef damage;
  • fish habitat loss;
  • community responsibility;
  • prevention and awareness.

C. Use Real Observation if Possible

Teachers may ask students to count plastic cups or snack wrappers in one safe area after break time. Use the data for writing practice.

D. Keep Debate Healthy

Remind students:

  • debate is not blaming;
  • every group has a role;
  • strong arguments need evidence;
  • respectful communication is part of learning.

E. Remedial Ideas

For students who struggle:

  1. provide sentence starters;
  2. let them write one argument paragraph first;
  3. use matching activities for thesis and arguments;
  4. let them present orally before writing;
  5. allow pair writing.

F. Enrichment Ideas

For advanced students:

  1. compare two exposition texts;
  2. use real class survey data;
  3. create an infographic;
  4. lead a mini campaign;
  5. write a speech for school assembly.

PART T — Visual Asset Plan for HTML Version

68. Visual Assets Needed

Visual Asset Description Pedagogical Function
Hero image School yard with plastic cups and snack wrappers after break time Hook and prediction
Close-up waste image Plastic cups and wrappers on school ground Vocabulary and observation
Waste-to-sea infographic Wind/rain/tide moving waste toward coast Cause-effect explanation
Healthy vs damaged reef Comparison of coral reef conditions Marine environment discussion
Destructive fishing impact illustration Shows damaged habitat without technical method Environmental awareness
Argument map Thesis → argument → evidence → explanation Text structure support
Debate scene Students discussing responsibility Speaking/debate model
Waste-count chart Sample plastic cup data Evidence practice
Zero waste poster School campaign design Project model
Tumbler campaign poster Reduce plastic cups Action-oriented task
Sea protection poster Protect coral and fish habitat Campaign project
Nominalisation card pollute → pollution, destroy → destruction Grammar support
Causal connector flowchart because → therefore → as a result Language support
Project display board Student campaign products Final presentation model

PART U — Closing Message for Students

69. Final Message

English is not only a subject. It is also a tool to speak about real problems around us. When students learn exposition text, they learn how to build opinions, support ideas with evidence, and invite people to think.

A plastic cup in the school yard may look small. A damaged coral reef may feel far from the classroom. But in island life, school, land, sea, and future are connected.

A clean school teaches responsibility. A healthy sea supports families. A strong argument can become a voice for change.

So remember:

Protect your school. Protect your sea. Protect your future.

End of Chapter 2
